did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used a Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) to detect melanoma from clinical images and lesion clinical information. The study addressed class imbalance using evolutionary algorithms (specifically Mixup Extrapolation Balancing) and weighted loss functions, achieving a balanced accuracy of 92% and recall of 94%.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving the development of a mobile application linked to a Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) for melanoma detection. It describes specific methods for handling imbalanced data (Mixup Extrapolation Balancing, weighted loss), details the dataset (PAD-UFES), and reports new experimental results (balanced accuracy of 92%, recall of 94%) obtained through simulations and cross-validation. The IEEE copyright notice (978-1-7281-6926-2/20) indicates it is a conference paper, which falls under the category of research papers producing new results.

title: An app to detect melanoma using deep learning: An approach to handle imbalanced data based on evolutionary algorithms
brief_summary: This study developed a mobile application to assist in melanoma detection by combining clinical images with patient lesion information using a Convolutional Neural Network (CNN). To address the class imbalance inherent in melanoma datasets, the authors employed an evolutionary algorithm-based technique called Mixup Extrapolation Balancing (MUPEB) alongside weighted loss functions and oversampling. The model was trained on the PAD-UFES dataset, which includes images of six common skin diseases plus an 'Others' category. The system architecture deploys the CNN on a server via a REST API, allowing a React Native mobile app to send image and clinical data for diagnosis. Experimental results demonstrated that the proposed approach achieved a balanced accuracy of 92% and a recall of 94%, outperforming standard balancing techniques. The authors conclude that integrating clinical information and handling data imbalance effectively improves automated melanoma detection performance.

lesion_types_studied.details: The primary target class for detection. The study focuses on distinguishing melanoma from non-melanoma lesions. It comprises 67 images in the PAD-UFES dataset.
A composite class used for binary classification against melanoma. It aggregates multiple specific lesion types totaling 1990 images in the PAD-UFES dataset.
One of the six most common skin diseases in the PAD-UFES dataset, included in the 'Non-melanoma' group for the binary classification task. Contains 543 images.
One of the six most common skin diseases in the PAD-UFES dataset, included in the 'Non-melanoma' group. Contains 442 images.
One of the six most common skin diseases in the PAD-UFES dataset, included in the 'Non-melanoma' group. Contains 196 images.
One of the six most common skin diseases in the PAD-UFES dataset, included in the 'Non-melanoma' group. Contains 149 images.
One of the six most common skin diseases in the PAD-UFES dataset, included in the 'Non-melanoma' group. Contains 215 images.
A category added to the original PAD-UFES dataset to include lesions not represented in the previous six classes. Included in the 'Non-melanoma' group. Contains 445 images.

all_task_types.target: Binary diagnosis of Melanoma vs. Non-melanoma
all_task_types.details: The model performs binary classification to detect melanoma from clinical images and lesion clinical information. The target classes are 'Melanoma' (67 images) and 'Non-melanoma' (1990 images, aggregating Actinic Keratosis, Basal Cell Carcinoma, Nevus, Squamous Cell Carcinoma, Seborrheic Keratosis, and Others). The study reports balanced accuracy (BACC), precision, recall, and F-measure as evaluation metrics for this classification task.

datasets_used.name: PAD-UFES
datasets_used.description: A dataset composed of clinical images collected from smartphones and lesion clinical information, originally introduced by Pacheco and Krohling [19] and expanded in this work to include an 'Others' class for lesions not represented in the six most common skin diseases.

use.training: Used to train the ResNet50 Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) via 5-fold cross-validation.
use.validation: Used for validation within the 5-fold cross-validation process.
use.testing: Used for testing/evaluation within the 5-fold cross-validation process.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To evaluate the performance of the ResNet50 model by splitting the PAD-UFES dataset into training and testing subsets iteratively.
To handle class imbalance by penalizing misclassification of minority classes (melanoma) more heavily during training.
To balance the dataset by creating copies of the minority class samples to match the majority class count.
To balance the dataset by generating new synthetic samples through mixup and extrapolation operations on existing images.
Used as a data balancing technique to generate new samples for the minority class.
To visualize high-dimensional features extracted by the CNN in two dimensions for qualitative assessment of class separation.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. It reported performance metrics (balanced accuracy, recall, precision, F-measure) with standard deviations from 5-fold cross-validation but did not perform statistical significance testing between methods.

PAD-UFES.notes: The weighted loss function (WGT) presented the best result in terms of recall. The Differential Evolution (DE) approach provided the best result in terms of balanced accuracy. Both WGT and DE presented significantly better results compared to baseline [14] with an increase of about 16% in recall and 6% in BACC. The proposed MUPEB and DE methods had lower performance compared to the weighted loss function.

architecture_used.pretraining: Not explicitly reported (referenced as effective performance in [19])

architecture_details.feature_combination: Combines features extracted from images (FI) and lesion clinical information (CI). Best setup used 70% FI and 30% CI.
architecture_details.clinical_feature_encoding: 22 variables: 15 bits for region of the body, 1 integer for age, and 6 bits for remaining features (patient's age, body part, itching, bleeding, hurting, recent increase, pattern change, elevation).
architecture_details.modification: Last layer modified for melanoma detection (binary classification).

training_details.learning_rate_schedule: Decreases by a factor of 1/2 or 1/5 every 20 epochs, alternately.
training_details.loss_function: Weighted cross-entropy loss function (for WGT approach); standard loss for others.

preprocessing_techniques: Clinical information encoding: 22 variables (15 bits for body region, 1 integer for age, 6 bits for remaining features like itching, bleeding, hurting, recent increase, pattern change, elevation)

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Applied during training; specific geometric or color transform parameters were not reported in the text.
Used as a data balancing technique by making copies of the training dataset until classes had the same number of samples.

Balanced the dataset by applying mixup and extrapolation operations. Generated 5 new images from pairs of images (X1, X2) using combinations: P1=0.5X1+0.5X2, P2=1.5X1+0.5X2, P3=0.5X1+1.5X2, P4=1.5X1-0.5X2, P5=1.5X2-0.5X1. Applied only to samples from the same class.

Used as a data balancing technique to generate new samples for the minority class. Combined 3 images (X1, X2, X3) into a new image using the formula X4 = X1 + α(X2 - X3), where α ranges from -0.5 to 0.5 chosen via uniform probability distribution. Applied only to samples from the same class.

conclusions: The study developed a multi-platform mobile application linked to a Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) to assist in melanoma detection by combining clinical images and lesion clinical information.
The proposed approach using an evolutionary algorithm (Differential Evolution mutation operator) to handle imbalanced data achieved a balanced accuracy of 92% and a recall of 94%.

Integrating lesion clinical information improved the model's performance, providing an average increase in balanced accuracy of 1.26% and recall of 11.43%, although it caused an average decrease in precision of 7.01%.
The weighted loss function method achieved the best recall (100%), while the Differential Evolution (DE) approach achieved the best balanced accuracy (92.39%).

The proposed Mixup Extrapolation Balancing (MUPEB) and Differential Evolution (DE) methods demonstrated significantly better results compared to baseline works, with an increase of about 16% in recall and 6% in balanced accuracy.

Both Mixup Extrapolation Balancing and Differential Evolution presented lower performance compared to the weighted loss function in terms of overall metrics, although DE results were very close to the weighted loss function.
The authors acknowledge that the study is preliminary and the reported metrics may vary when applied to other datasets or real-case applications due to the small size of the dataset.
important_remarks: The study is explicitly described as preliminary; the authors acknowledge that reported metrics may vary significantly in real-case applications due to the small dataset size (only 67 melanoma images).

The dataset (PAD-UFES) consists of clinical images collected from smartphones rather than standardized dermoscopy images, which introduces potential variability in image quality and lighting conditions compared to controlled clinical settings.

The model relies on server-side deployment via a REST API rather than on-device inference, which raises concerns about latency, internet connectivity requirements, and patient data privacy during transmission.

The precision for the best-performing model (Differential Evolution) was low (35.67 ± 22.64), indicating a high false positive rate where many non-melanoma lesions are incorrectly classified as melanoma.

The study lacks external validation; performance was evaluated solely using 5-fold cross-validation on a single, small, local dataset (PAD-UFES), limiting generalizability to other populations or imaging devices.

No statistical significance testing (e.g., p-values) was performed to compare the proposed balancing techniques against baselines or each other; comparisons were based on mean metric values and standard deviations.

The 'Non-melanoma' class is a composite of five distinct skin diseases plus an 'Others' category, which may obscure specific diagnostic challenges between different benign and malignant non-melanoma lesions.
